general_concerns: Believes the problem is mainly 'being allergic to everything' and that infections happen because she 'didn’t avoid triggers' rather than an immune system disorder.
Thinks high IgE means she has 'too much immunity' and that immune treatments will 'shut down' her immune system permanently.
Assumes antibiotics should be avoided unless she has a high fever because 'antibiotics weaken your immune system' and 'make you dependent.'
Believes recurrent skin and lung infections are mostly due to 'poor hygiene' and is embarrassed, so she downplays symptoms and delays care.
Thinks vaccines are unsafe for her because her immune system is 'fragile,' and assumes all vaccines are live vaccines.
Attributes symptoms to stress, diet, or mold exposure alone and expects that moving homes or taking supplements will fix the condition.
Believes genetic conditions are always inherited from a parent who is sick, so she doubts a recessive diagnosis if parents appear healthy.
economic_concerns: Worries that seeing specialists and getting lab tests will create bills she cannot pay, so she avoids follow-up and says she is 'fine.'
Thinks prophylactic medications are optional because 'daily meds are a luxury' and tries to stretch prescriptions by skipping doses.
Fears losing her job if she discloses frequent infections or needs time off, so she minimizes symptoms and avoids documentation.
Believes home remedies and over-the-counter supplements are cheaper and 'safer' than prescribed therapies, even when infections recur.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 19-year-old woman who is vague and a bit evasive. Start by saying you keep getting “nasty” skin infections (painful swollen boils that sometimes drain pus) and chest infections (cough every few weeks with thick mucus) and that antibiotics help briefly then the infections return. Also mention itchy, dry skin patches that flare.

Only share details if directly asked: fevers with infections; sometimes lumps don’t feel very hot; shortness of breath when sick and easy fatigue; frequent sinus pressure with bad-smelling drainage; childhood ear infections and current ear fullness/ringing; mouth sores/gum irritation; loose stools after antibiotics and occasional cramps; parents are cousins; a sibling had “immune problems.”

Do not volunteer: childhood “weird” lung infections, occasional coughing blood, prior scans showing lung “pockets/scars,” early tooth loss, poor vaccine response.

Resist bringing up genital sores unless specifically asked about genital symptoms/sexual health; then admit embarrassment and negative STI tests.

Express misconceptions: “Maybe it’s MRSA,” “maybe asthma/allergies,” worry about HIV. Minimize follow-up due to cost/job fears and admit stretching/skipping daily meds. If met with empathy, open up slightly but stay cautious.

comorbidities: Recurrent skin abscesses and pneumonias with bronchiectasis
confounders: Severe atopic dermatitis with very high IgE can mimic primary immunodeficiency severity
Chronic inhaled corticosteroid use may increase risk of oral candidiasis and confound recurrent infection assessment
Bronchiectasis predisposes to recurrent bacterial colonization/infection independent of immune defect

diagnosis.primary: hyper-IgE recurrent infection syndrome 5, autosomal recessive

correct_diagnosis.primary: Hyper-IgE recurrent infection syndrome 5, autosomal recessive (DOCK8 deficiency)
acceptable_alternatives: Severe atopic dermatitis with secondary infection
required_evidence: Markedly elevated total IgE (typically >2000 IU/mL) with eczema/atopy phenotype
Peripheral eosinophilia
History of recurrent sinopulmonary and/or skin infections (often staphylococcal) and/or severe/recurrent viral skin infections (molluscum, HSV, HPV) consistent with DOCK8 deficiency
Inflammatory markers (CRP/ESR) may rise during acute infection but can be normal between episodes
CBC may show eosinophilia; neutrophils often normal unless acute bacterial infection
Clinical context consistent with autosomal recessive hyper-IgE syndrome (e.g., severe allergies, viral infections, malignancy risk) rather than STAT3 features (skeletal/dental findings)

expected_lab_results.Total IgE: Markedly elevated (often >2000 IU/mL; may be many thousands)
expected_lab_results.CBC WBC: Often normal to elevated during infection; may show leukocytosis with acute bacterial infection
expected_lab_results.Neutrophils (absolute): Usually normal; may be elevated with acute bacterial infection
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Usually normal; may be low if chronic inflammation/infection
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Usually normal; may be reactive thrombocytosis with inflammation
expected_lab_results.C-reactive protein (CRP): Normal between infections; elevated during acute bacterial infection
expected_lab_results.Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR): Normal between infections; elevated during active inflammation/infection

medications: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP component) 5 mg/kg/day PO divided BID for antibacterial prophylaxis
Acyclovir 400 mg PO BID for herpesvirus prophylaxis if recurrent HSV/VZV or significant viral skin disease
IVIG 400-600 mg/kg IV every 3-4 weeks if recurrent/severe infections or poor specific antibody responses
Topical corticosteroid (e.g., triamcinolone 0.1% ointment) apply thin layer to eczematous areas BID for flares
Mupirocin 2% ointment apply to localized impetiginized lesions TID for 5-7 days as needed
Cetirizine 10 mg PO daily for pruritus/allergic symptoms
non_pharmacological: Urgent referral to Clinical Immunology for definitive workup (including genetic confirmation if not already confirmed) and comprehensive immune phenotyping
Discuss and refer early for hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) evaluation as definitive therapy for DOCK8 deficiency
Skin care regimen: daily emollients, trigger avoidance, bleach baths 1-2 times weekly if recurrent staphylococcal skin infections (per dermatology/immunology guidance)
Infection prevention: prompt evaluation of fevers, low threshold for cultures and imaging for suspected pneumonia/abscess; avoid exposure to sick contacts when possible
Vaccination strategy: avoid live vaccines until immunology review; ensure inactivated vaccines are up to date; household contacts vaccinated appropriately
Malignancy surveillance awareness (increased risk with DOCK8): counsel to report persistent lymphadenopathy, weight loss, night sweats, nonhealing skin lesions
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Immunology follow-up within 1-2 weeks; sooner (24-72 hours) if active infection, worsening symptoms, or abnormal vitals; ongoing monitoring every 1-3 months depending on severity

must_include: This condition is an inherited immune deficiency (autosomal recessive hyper-IgE syndrome/DOCK8 deficiency) that causes very high IgE, eczema/allergies, and recurrent infections
Expected infection patterns and why infections can be frequent/severe (skin, lungs; often bacterial and viral skin infections)
Need for specialist confirmation and ongoing immune monitoring; possible curative option with HSCT
Rationale for antimicrobial prophylaxis and when to seek urgent care (fever, breathing difficulty, rapidly spreading skin infection)
Skin-directed therapy plan (emollients, anti-inflammatory topical therapy, treating secondary infection) and adherence expectations
If IVIG is recommended: purpose, schedule, common side effects, and monitoring
Review allergy history: amoxicillin-clavulanate caused urticarial rash; avoid this drug and discuss safe alternatives for bacterial infections
Discuss risks/benefits and uncertainties (infection risk, viral disease, malignancy risk; HSCT considerations)
Shared decision-making and planning for rapid access to care during infections
Infection prevention strategies (hand hygiene, early evaluation of fevers, wound care, avoiding live vaccines until cleared)
Eczema trigger management and consistent moisturization
Family counseling: genetic implications, offer genetic counseling for patient/family planning

Close with safety-net instructions and confirmation of understanding.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 22-year-old anxious but clear-speaking college student working part-time retail. Start with: “I keep getting big bruises for no reason, and one of them won’t go away.” Volunteer: large purple bruises on arms/legs without remembering trauma, they spread/last long, and gums bleed when brushing. If asked about bleeding history, share: weekly nosebleeds that take a while to stop; small cuts ooze; prolonged bleeding after tooth extraction; heavy periods since menarche with clots and frequent pad changes; big bruises/bleeding after shots or blood draws. Do not mention unless specifically probed: bruises after carrying groceries/tight backpack; childhood easy bruising; avoiding ibuprofen/aspirin because bleeding seems worse; increasing fatigue from heavy periods. Resist bringing up bleeding after sex and blood in urine after hard workouts—only admit if asked directly, embarrassed and scared. Express worries: night fevers/sweats and leukemia concern; shortness of breath/pallor and anemia; occasional joint aches and fear of “joint bleeds like hemophilia.” Ask about rare factor medicine (catridecacog) coverage; believe you should ration doses and think only the ED can give factor, fearing costs. If clinician shows empathy, soften, thank them, and share more details.

Agent performs a drug interaction check before prescribing (non-critical), including OTC agents (aspirin/NSAIDs), anticoagulants, antiplatelets, and herbal supplements that may affect bleeding.

Agent addresses family implications and offers genetic counseling/testing options.
Agent avoids unsafe or incorrect recommendations (e.g., initiating anticoagulants, suggesting aspirin for pain without caveat, or dismissing bleeding red flags).
Agent’s plan is coherent: no prescribing occurs before required safety checks; follow-up is scheduled with hematology and monitoring is specified.

Arterial blood gas on room air showing hypoxemia (low PaO2 and low SaO2) often with respiratory alkalosis early (low PaCO2)
No clear alternative cause after evaluation (no cardiogenic pulmonary edema, no obvious infection/aspiration, no toxic/drug exposure explaining findings)

expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (room air) - HCO3-: Normal to mildly low (appropriate compensation for respiratory alkalosis)
expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (room air) - SaO2: Low (often <90% on room air depending on severity)
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Normal to elevated (stress leukocytosis possible; does not exclude infection)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal to mildly low (can be normal early; thrombocytopenia would raise concern for sepsis/DIC if present)

Enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily for VTE prophylaxis (or heparin 5000 units SC q8-12h if renal impairment/bleeding risk assessment supports)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit acute respiratory history, severity, timeline, triggers/exposures, infectious symptoms, cardiogenic symptoms, bleeding risk, and baseline function to frame differential (AIP/ARDS vs infection, PE, CHF, ILD flare, inhalational injury).
Confirm medication list (including propranolol), allergy details (penicillin urticarial rash), and comorbidities/pregnancy/immune status to guide safe prescribing and contraindication screening.

Order initial workup for hypoxemic respiratory failure and to exclude mimics: ABG/VBG, CBC, CMP, lactate, CRP/procalcitonin, blood cultures if febrile, respiratory viral PCR, sputum culture if productive, BNP/troponin as indicated, D-dimer/CTPA pathway if PE suspected.
Order imaging and monitoring: chest X-ray (and CT chest if available/appropriate), ECG, continuous pulse oximetry; consider echocardiography to evaluate cardiogenic edema if unclear.
Interpret labs/imaging to assess severity (PaO2/FiO2), bilateral infiltrates, absence of cardiogenic cause, and lack of clear infectious etiology; identify need for ICU-level support.

Make/justify working diagnosis of acute interstitial pneumonia (idiopathic diffuse alveolar damage) after rapid course + imaging and exclusion of infection, heart failure, exposures/CTD/drug-induced causes; document uncertainty and differential.
Perform required critical safety check before any prescribing; verify penicillin allergy phenotype (urticarial rash) and severity to inform antibiotic options if infection coverage is initiated.

Perform required critical interaction check before prescribing (e.g., steroids, empiric antibiotics, sedation/analgesia if ventilated) and specifically consider propranolol interactions/physiologic effects.
If additional meds are considered (e.g., anticoagulation for VTE prophylaxis, PPI for stress ulcer prophylaxis), re-check interactions as regimen evolves.

Initiate supportive treatment: oxygen escalation plan (including high-flow), ICU consult/transfer, lung-protective ventilation strategy if intubated; consider corticosteroids only after reasonable infection evaluation; order prophylaxis measures when indicated.

Communicate diagnosis, uncertainty, potential rapid deterioration/intubation, treatment plan and monitoring, and address patient concerns including penicillin allergy implications and propranolol considerations.

Plan reassessment cadence (repeat ABG/oxygenation checks), inpatient specialty involvement (ICU/pulm), and post-discharge follow-up with return precautions; include lifestyle/exposure avoidance and vaccination review during recovery planning.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, questions answered, and safety-netting provided.

minimal_paths.description: Core path: assess acute hypoxemic respiratory failure, order key tests to exclude common causes, diagnose AIP as working diagnosis, complete mandatory safety checks, initiate supportive/ICU-oriented management, and communicate clearly.
must_collect: onset/timeline of dyspnea (days to weeks) and progression
severity markers: rest dyspnea, inability to speak full sentences, syncope/altered mentation
oxygenation status (SpO2/need for supplemental O2) and work of breathing
autoimmune symptoms (rash, arthralgias, Raynaud) when relevant
past lung disease and baseline functional status
penicillin allergy details (urticarial rash, timing, severity/anaphylaxis history)
current medications including propranolol and indication
steps: ASK: focused history for acute hypoxemic respiratory failure + differential
ORDER_LAB: ABG/VBG, CBC, CMP, inflammatory markers, cultures/viral testing as indicated
ORDER_LAB: CXR ± CT chest; ECG; consider BNP/echo depending on presentation
GET_RESULTS: interpret severity and exclusion of cardiogenic edema/infection when possible
DIAGNOSE: working AIP after exclusion framing; note uncertainty and differential
CHECK_INTERACTION: review propranolol and planned meds
PRESCRIBE: oxygen escalation/ICU consult; supportive ARDS-style care; consider steroids with caveats; prophylaxis when indicated
EDUCATE: diagnosis + treatment + concerns + lifestyle/return precautions
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: reassessment plan and post-discharge follow-up

required_evidence: Known diagnosis of sickle cell anemia (HbSS) or strong history consistent with SCD

expected_lab_results.WBC: Normal to mildly elevated (baseline leukocytosis is common in SCD; should not be severely low on stable hydroxyurea)
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Normal to elevated (reactive thrombocytosis can occur; should not be severely low on stable hydroxyurea)
expected_lab_results.Direct bilirubin: Normal or mildly elevated; indirect fraction predominates (consistent with hemolysis)

diagnosis.primary: congenital myasthenic syndromes with glycosylation defect

correct_diagnosis.primary: Congenital myasthenic syndrome due to glycosylation defect (e.g., GMPPB-related CMS)
acceptable_alternatives: GFPT1-related congenital myasthenic syndrome (limb-girdle CMS with glycosylation defect)
required_evidence: Longstanding/familial or childhood-onset fatigable weakness with fluctuating severity (ocular, bulbar, limb, or respiratory involvement)
Electrophysiology supportive of neuromuscular junction transmission defect (e.g., decrement on low-frequency repetitive nerve stimulation and/or increased jitter on single-fiber EMG)
Absence of autoimmune myasthenia features (e.g., negative AChR/MuSK/LRP4 antibodies if tested; lack of thymoma history) and/or poor fit for acquired MG

Genetic confirmation of a glycosylation-pathway CMS gene (e.g., GMPPB, GFPT1, DPAGT1) OR a phenotype strongly suggestive of glycosylation-defect CMS with response pattern consistent with CMS therapies
Clinical history of medication-triggered worsening (e.g., macrolide-associated worsening) consistent with neuromuscular junction vulnerability

expected_lab_results.CMP - Bicarbonate (CO2): Usually normal; may be low if acute hyperventilation or high if chronic hypoventilation with CO2 retention (not expected without respiratory failure)

medications: Pyridostigmine 30-60 mg PO every 4-6 hours while awake (titrate to effect and tolerability; avoid overtreatment)
3,4-diaminopyridine (amifampridine) 5 mg PO three times daily, increase by 5 mg per dose every 3-4 days as tolerated to 10-20 mg PO three to four times daily (max 80 mg/day; specialist-guided)
Albuterol (salbutamol) 2 mg PO three times daily (or 4 mg PO twice daily) as adjunct if inadequate response to above; monitor heart rate and potassium
non_pharmacological: Avoid medications known to worsen neuromuscular transmission (especially macrolides, fluoroquinolones, aminoglycosides, magnesium-containing products, and neuromuscular blockers when possible)
Respiratory assessment if bulbar/respiratory symptoms: bedside forced vital capacity/negative inspiratory force when symptomatic; low threshold for ED evaluation if dyspnea, orthopnea, or weak cough
Swallow safety evaluation if choking/aspiration symptoms; diet texture modification as needed
Physical therapy/energy conservation strategies; pacing activity to reduce fatigability
Genetic counseling and family screening discussion once genotype confirmed
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neuromuscular specialist follow-up within 2-4 weeks after medication changes (earlier within 48-72 hours if respiratory/bulbar symptoms or rapid deterioration); reassess efficacy and adverse effects and adjust regimen

must_include: This is an inherited disorder of neuromuscular transmission (not autoimmune myasthenia)
Symptoms fluctuate and worsen with fatigue/illness; can involve breathing and swallowing
Glycosylation-defect CMS is due to genetic changes affecting proteins at the neuromuscular junction; genetic confirmation guides therapy and family counseling
Goal is to improve neuromuscular transmission and reduce fatigable weakness; response varies by subtype
How to take pyridostigmine (timing, expected benefit, common side effects such as abdominal cramps/diarrhea; signs of cholinergic excess like increased secretions, worsening weakness)
If using amifampridine: gradual titration, seizure risk at high doses/predisposition, and need to avoid interacting QT-prolonging drugs where possible
If using albuterol: possible tremor/palpitations and need for monitoring (heart rate, potassium if symptomatic)
Explicit warning that azithromycin (macrolides) previously worsened weakness/shortness of breath and should be avoided unless specialist-directed
Penicillin allergy history (urticarial rash) should be documented; discuss alternatives if antibiotics are needed
Red-flag symptoms requiring urgent care: increasing shortness of breath, inability to count/breathe comfortably, weak cough, choking/aspiration, rapidly worsening weakness
Avoid triggers: infections, overheating, sleep deprivation, and contraindicated medications/supplements (including magnesium-containing laxatives/antacids)
Plan for procedures/anesthesia: inform anesthesiology about CMS; increased sensitivity to neuromuscular blockers and respiratory depressants
Create an emergency plan/medical alert information listing diagnosis and drugs to avoid

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current symptoms (fatigable weakness pattern), bulbar/respiratory involvement, fluctuation with illness/fatigue, current meds/supplements (esp magnesium), prior antibiotic reactions (azithromycin worsening), seizure/cardiac history, pregnancy status if relevant, and family history/genetic testing status.

Order baseline and safety labs as appropriate for planned therapy and differential/safety (e.g., CMP/electrolytes incl potassium if beta-agonist planned; consider ECG if amifampridine/QT risk; consider CK/TSH/B12 if alternative etiologies need exclusion).

Review results to ensure no correctable contributors (electrolyte abnormalities) and to support safe initiation/monitoring of selected therapy (e.g., potassium, renal/hepatic function; ECG if obtained).

State working diagnosis: congenital myasthenic syndrome due to glycosylation defect (inherited NMJ transmission disorder), and note need for genetic confirmation/subtype to guide therapy and counseling.
Perform allergy check before any prescribing; document penicillin allergy (urticarial rash) and prior macrolide-associated clinical worsening.

Check interactions before prescribing, including QT-prolonging agents (relevant if amifampridine), medications that worsen neuromuscular transmission (e.g., macrolides, fluoroquinolones, aminoglycosides, magnesium), and patient’s current meds/supplements.

Confirm no contraindications before prescribing (e.g., seizure disorder for amifampridine; significant arrhythmia/QT risk; asthma/cardiac issues for beta-agonists; GI/urinary obstruction considerations for pyridostigmine).

Initiate evidence-aligned symptomatic therapy tailored to subtype/response history (often trial pyridostigmine; consider amifampridine and/or albuterol in appropriate CMS glycosylation-defect subtypes), with titration plan and monitoring instructions.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis (inherited, not autoimmune), explain treatment options and side effects/risks, address medication avoidance (azithromycin/macrolides; magnesium), red flags, lifestyle/procedure planning, and emergency plan/medical alert.
Arrange close follow-up to assess response/tolerability, adjust dosing, review genetic testing/referral, and set monitoring (vitals, potassium if beta-agonist symptoms, ECG if QT risk).
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

Don’t mention accidents, sex-life impact, avoiding meetings/car rides, irritability without water, racing heart when very thirsty, dry skin/cracked lips, or craving cold water unless directly asked—and even then, minimize embarrassment.

If asked about timeline, say it’s been gradual over a couple months and worsening. If asked about urine, say it stays very pale/clear even in the morning and you still pee a lot even if you stop drinking briefly. Deny burning, fever. Mention occasional lightheadedness and a dull headache behind the eyes if prompted.

Express misconceptions: worry it’s diabetes; wonder if you “caused it” by drinking water because it’s healthy; assume it’s related to a congenital urinary blockage; caffeine cut didn’t help. Be anxious about cost, ask for one prescription without repeated labs, suggest OTC electrolyte drinks, and avoid discussing job impact. If the clinician shows empathy, soften slightly, but stay guarded about work and embarrassment.

expected_lab_results.Serum sodium: Often high-normal to elevated (e.g., >145 mmol/L) if free water intake is insufficient; may be normal if compensatory polydipsia
expected_lab_results.Serum osmolality: Normal-high to elevated (often >295 mOsm/kg) when water intake does not match losses
expected_lab_results.Urine osmolality: Low (often <300 mOsm/kg; frequently <200 mOsm/kg in complete cases)
expected_lab_results.Plasma copeptin (baseline): Low or inappropriately low (commonly <2.6 pmol/L in complete central DI; interpret with clinical context and assay)

medications: Desmopressin 10 mcg intranasal twice daily (titrate to lowest effective dose)
OR Desmopressin 0.1 mg orally twice daily (titrate; oral often requires higher doses than intranasal)

non_pharmacological: Ensure free access to water; drink to thirst (unless impaired thirst/limited access—then create scheduled intake plan)

Avoid excessive desmopressin with overdrinking to reduce hyponatremia risk; consider intermittent dosing or occasional dose delay to allow aquaresis if prone to hyponatremia
Assess and address underlying etiology (e.g., pituitary/hypothalamic pathology): arrange pituitary MRI and endocrine evaluation (anterior pituitary hormones) if not already done
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1–2 weeks after starting or changing desmopressin (earlier in high-risk patients), then every 3–6 months once stable; check serum sodium sooner (e.g., 3–7 days) after initiation/titration or during intercurrent illness

must_include: Condition is due to insufficient antidiuretic hormone (vasopressin) production/release leading to inability to concentrate urine

Dose is individualized; aim for symptom control without causing water retention

When to seek urgent care: severe confusion, seizures, inability to keep fluids down, severe weakness, syncope

Maintain access to water and avoid both dehydration and overdrinking while on desmopressin
Keep a log of urine volume, thirst, weight, and dosing during titration
Inform clinicians before surgeries/procedures; ensure perioperative fluid and desmopressin plan

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key symptoms, onset, triggers, fluid intake, urine volume/nocturia, neurologic history (pituitary disease/trauma/surgery), pregnancy status, and red flags (dehydration, confusion). Order diagnostic labs to support CDI and assess safety before therapy: BMP (Na/K/Cr/glucose), serum osmolality, urine osmolality, urine specific gravity, and consider morning cortisol/TSH if pituitary disease suspected. If unstable/severe hypernatremia, prioritize urgent evaluation.

Make or confirm working diagnosis of central diabetes insipidus based on history and lab pattern (inappropriately dilute urine with high/normal serum osmolality and/or hypernatremia), and differentiate from diabetes mellitus and primary polydipsia.
Non-critical safety check prior to prescribing: verify no allergy to desmopressin or formulation components.
Critical safety check prior to prescribing: screen for medications increasing hyponatremia/SIADH risk (e.g., SSRIs/SNRIs, TCAs, carbamazepine, chlorpropamide, NSAIDs) and adjust monitoring/plan.

Critical contraindication-style check prior to prescribing: ensure no conditions/contexts that make desmopressin unsafe without specialist input (current or prior hyponatremia, polydipsia, significant renal impairment, uncontrolled heart failure, inability to restrict fluids, or acute illness with vomiting/diarrhea).
Initiate desmopressin with individualized dosing and clear titration/monitoring plan; include follow-up sodium monitoring and instructions to avoid overdrinking.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment, safety (hyponatremia/water intoxication), sick-day rules, urgent return precautions, and lifestyle/logging guidance including peri-procedural planning.
Arrange close follow-up for symptom response and sodium checks (early after initiation/titration), and longer-term endocrinology coordination; ensure access/adherence plan.
Close encounter after confirming patient understanding and safety-netting.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 27-year-old elementary school teacher. You are anxious but speak clearly and directly. Start with: “I’ve been feeling wiped out for weeks, and I keep getting bruises and nosebleeds out of nowhere.” Early in the visit, volunteer: extreme fatigue/weakness despite rest, easy bruising, nosebleeds that take a long time to stop, shortness of breath on stairs, and looking pale.

If asked about other symptoms, disclose: more frequent/lingering colds and sore throats, low-grade fevers with chills, gum bleeding with brushing, heavier periods (or prolonged bleeding after shaving), dizziness on standing, more headaches/pressure, and mild unintentional weight loss with low appetite.

Do not volunteer unless specifically asked/examined: pinpoint non-blanching spots on legs, slow-oozing cuts, avoiding crowds, racing heart at rest, brittle nails and craving ice.

Resist mentioning blood when wiping after bowel movements and painful mouth sores unless asked gently; admit embarrassment. Mention worry that bruises look suspicious.

Express misconceptions: “It’s probably stress/low iron/mono/new workout.” Say you tried iron and OTC immune boosters. Ask about costs, insurance, and fear of job/coverage loss. If the clinician shows empathy, you calm slightly, thank them, and become more forthcoming.

Peripheral smear.value: Macrocytosis; markedly decreased platelets; leukopenia; no schistocytes; no blasts seen

Bone marrow biopsy (summary).value: Hypocellular marrow (~15% cellularity) with trilineage hypoplasia; no excess blasts; no dysplasia; cytogenetics pending
Bone marrow biopsy (summary).normal_range: Normocellular marrow (~40-60% cellularity) with normal trilineage hematopoiesis

Norethindrone 0.35 mg PO daily
comorbidities: Transfusion-dependent anemia with secondary iron overload
confounders: Chronic transfusion history (can mask marrow production indices and elevate ferritin independent of inflammation)
Recent viral upper respiratory illness (raises concern for transient aplastic crisis vs evolving aplastic anemia)
Chronic prednisone use (can blunt fever/inflammatory response and increase infection risk)
Iron overload with mild transaminitis (can complicate interpretation of fatigue and lab abnormalities)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Aplastic anemia (acquired bone marrow failure syndrome)
acceptable_alternatives: Bone marrow failure syndrome, unspecified (pending marrow biopsy)
Hypoproliferative pancytopenia consistent with aplastic anemia
required_evidence: Anemia with inappropriately low reticulocyte count (reticulocytopenia) indicating decreased marrow production
Normocytic or mildly macrocytic indices with elevated RDW possible
Leukopenia on CBC (WBC low) supporting marrow failure pattern (platelets not provided but would often be low)
No laboratory pattern suggesting hemolysis (reticulocytes not elevated) or acute blood loss
Plan for confirmatory bone marrow evaluation (hypocellular marrow) and exclusion of reversible causes (e.g., B12/folate deficiency, drugs, viral hepatitis/HIV, PNH)

expected_lab_results.MCV: Normal to mildly high (normocytic to mild macrocytosis)
expected_lab_results.Absolute reticulocyte count: Low (inappropriately low for degree of anemia)

medications: Packed red blood cells 1 unit IV PRN symptomatic anemia or Hb threshold per protocol (e.g., <7-8 g/dL; higher if cardiac disease)
Cyclosporine 5 mg/kg/day PO in 2 divided doses (adjust to trough levels and renal function)
Eltrombopag 150 mg PO daily (dose-adjust for hepatic impairment and East Asian ancestry per labeling; separate from polyvalent cations)
Horse antithymocyte globulin (ATG) 40 mg/kg/day IV daily x 4 days with premedication (e.g., methylprednisolone, acetaminophen, antihistamine) and serum sickness prophylaxis per protocol
non_pharmacological: Urgent hematology consultation; arrange bone marrow aspirate/biopsy with cytogenetics/flow and evaluation for PNH clone
Supportive care: infection precautions if neutropenic; prompt evaluation/empiric antibiotics for fever per neutropenic fever protocol
Avoid myelotoxic medications and unnecessary radiation/chemical exposures; review all current meds and supplements
Transfusion strategy: leukoreduced and irradiated blood products if candidate for transplant/immunosuppression; minimize donor exposures when possible
Consider HLA typing and referral for allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant evaluation (especially younger patients or matched sibling donor availability)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Hematology follow-up within 24-72 hours (or inpatient management if severe); repeat CBC/reticulocytes at least weekly initially and after therapy initiation per hematology protocol

must_include: Bone marrow is not making enough blood cells (low production) leading to anemia and often low white cells/platelets
Reticulocyte count is low, supporting decreased production rather than bleeding or hemolysis
Need confirmatory bone marrow biopsy and tests to rule out other causes (nutritional deficiencies, infections, medication/toxin effects, PNH)
Supportive transfusions may be needed for symptoms/low hemoglobin
Definitive therapy depends on severity and donor options: stem cell transplant vs immunosuppressive therapy (ATG + cyclosporine ± eltrombopag)
Expected timeline: response to immunosuppression may take weeks to months; close monitoring is required
Review penicillin allergy and ensure antibiotics chosen for fever/infection are safe alternatives if needed
Discuss key risks: infection/bleeding risk from low counts, infusion reactions/serum sickness with ATG, kidney/blood pressure effects with cyclosporine, liver/clot risks with eltrombopag
Clear return precautions: fever, bleeding/bruising, shortness of breath, chest pain, severe fatigue
Avoid NSAIDs/aspirin unless instructed (bleeding risk if platelets low—often present in marrow failure)
Hand hygiene and avoid sick contacts if white count is low; seek urgent care for fever
Avoid new over-the-counter/herbal products without checking due to interaction/toxicity risk

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit symptoms and timeline suggestive of marrow failure (fatigue, dyspnea, infections/fevers, bruising/bleeding), and assess acuity/red flags.

Identify secondary causes and exposures: medication history (including recent antibiotics/antithyroid/antiepileptics), chemo/radiation, toxins (benzene), alcohol, travel, viral symptoms, autoimmune history, family history/inherited marrow failure.
Required before any prescribing; confirm penicillin allergy details and severity to guide safe empiric antibiotics if fever/neutropenia occurs.

Order initial diagnostic labs: CBC with differential, reticulocyte count, peripheral smear, CMP/LFTs, LDH, haptoglobin, bilirubin, iron studies, B12/folate, TSH as appropriate, viral serologies (HIV, hepatitis, parvovirus B19), and PNH flow cytometry; type and screen if transfusion likely.
Interpret results for pancytopenia pattern, low reticulocyte count (hypoproliferation), smear findings, and rule-in/rule-out hemolysis/nutritional deficiency/infection.
Order confirmatory evaluation: bone marrow aspirate/biopsy with cytogenetics/flow (to distinguish aplastic anemia vs MDS/leukemia), and consider HLA typing if severe aplastic anemia suspected.
Required before prescribing; check interactions for planned therapies (e.g., cyclosporine with azoles/macrolides, eltrombopag with polyvalent cations) and supportive meds.
Required before prescribing; re-check interactions if any new medication is selected (e.g., antimicrobial choice in neutropenic fever).
Required before ordering; ensure no contraindications to planned procedures/orders (e.g., biopsy considerations in severe thrombocytopenia and need for platelet support, infection precautions).
Synthesize findings to diagnose suspected bone marrow failure syndrome (e.g., aplastic anemia) and document differential (MDS, leukemia, nutritional deficiency, infection, drug/toxin effect, PNH).

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis (low production, low retic, need biopsy and rule-outs), explain treatment options and timelines, address concerns/risks and return precautions, and provide lifestyle/safety guidance.

Required before prescribing; confirm no contraindications to any chosen therapy (e.g., cyclosporine renal dysfunction/uncontrolled HTN, ATG hypersensitivity risk, eltrombopag hepatic disease/thrombosis risk) and to transfusion strategy.

Initiate appropriate supportive/definitive management within scope: transfusion plan if symptomatic anemia/very low Hb, infection prophylaxis/urgent fever plan if neutropenic, and referral/plan for transplant vs immunosuppression (ATG + cyclosporine ± eltrombopag) as indicated.
Arrange urgent hematology follow-up, monitoring plan for counts and treatment toxicities, and timing for biopsy results review.
Close encounter after ensuring understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 56-year-old male auto repair shop owner. You speak vaguely and are somewhat evasive; you don’t like “too many tests” and worry about costs and losing work. Start with: “I’ve been having a lot of watery diarrhea and I’m losing weight.” If asked open-endedly, volunteer: watery diarrhea several times/day even with little food, unintentional weight loss, poor appetite, thirst/dry mouth, fatigue. Only if specifically asked, add: diarrhea also happens at night and wakes you; crampy belly pain relieved by bowel movements; lightheaded on standing; occasional racing heart on bad days; muscle weakness/cramps; peeing less on worst days; tried cutting dairy/gluten without benefit; OTC anti-diarrheals only briefly help. Keep hidden unless directly asked about nerves/mental status/GERD/dehydration: tingling/numb hands, heavy weak legs on stairs, occasional confusion/foggy when dehydrated, more heartburn/sour taste, skin less stretchy/drawn. Resist admitting accidents, food avoidance, and low libido unless asked gently and privately. Offer misconceptions: “Probably IBS from stress,” “maybe food poisoning/bug,” “could be my magnesium supplements.” If clinician shows empathy, soften slightly, admit fear about costs/insurance, but still prefer delaying evaluation.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Pancreatic polypeptide-secreting neuroendocrine tumor (PPoma)
acceptable_alternatives: Nonfunctional pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor (PanNET) with elevated pancreatic polypeptide
Pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor (well-differentiated) presenting with anorexia/weight loss
required_evidence: Markedly elevated fasting plasma pancreatic polypeptide level (typically several-fold above upper limit of normal) on at least one confirmatory repeat sample
Neuroendocrine tumor biochemical support (elevated chromogranin A, acknowledging possible false positives such as PPI use/renal impairment)
Clinical picture compatible with PPoma (often nonspecific symptoms such as poor appetite, weight loss, abdominal discomfort; absence of a classic functional syndrome)
Exclusion of insulinoma (no inappropriate hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia: fasting glucose not low with inappropriately high insulin/C-peptide/proinsulin)
Exclusion of gastrinoma (fasting gastrin not markedly elevated in the appropriate clinical context)

expected_lab_results.fasting_plasma_glucose: Normal or mildly elevated; not low (no fasting hypoglycemia)
expected_lab_results.hemoglobin_a1c: Normal or mildly elevated (may reflect stress hyperglycemia/diabetes if present, but not diagnostic)
expected_lab_results.serum_insulin_(fasting): Normal or low-normal (not inappropriately high for glucose level)
expected_lab_results.c_peptide_(fasting): Normal or low-normal (not inappropriately high for glucose level)
expected_lab_results.proinsulin: Normal (not elevated as would be expected in insulinoma)
expected_lab_results.plasma_pancreatic_polypeptide_(fasting): Markedly elevated (several-fold above upper limit of normal), persistent on repeat testing
expected_lab_results.chromogranin_a: Elevated (supportive of neuroendocrine tumor; interpret with caution if on PPIs or with renal dysfunction)
expected_lab_results.gastrin_(fasting): Normal or only mildly elevated (not consistent with gastrinoma)

medications: Octreotide LAR 20-30 mg IM every 4 weeks
Octreotide (short-acting) 100-200 mcg SC three times daily as bridge for symptom/biochemical control until LAR effective (optional based on symptoms and clinician preference)
non_pharmacological: Urgent referral to multidisciplinary neuroendocrine tumor team (endocrinology/oncology/surgical oncology)
Localize and stage disease with appropriate imaging (e.g., contrast-enhanced CT/MRI pancreas and liver; somatostatin receptor imaging such as Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CT) prior to definitive therapy
Surgical evaluation for resection if localized/resectable; consider cytoreductive approaches if metastatic and appropriate
Nutritional assessment and support for poor appetite; screen for weight loss and sarcopenia; consider dietitian referral
Assess for MEN1 if clinically indicated (personal/family history of hyperparathyroidism, pituitary tumors, other PanNETs)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 4-6 weeks after initiating somatostatin analog (earlier if worsening intake/weight loss), then every 3-6 months with symptom review and biomarker/imaging trend as directed by NET team

must_include: PPoma is a pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor that secretes pancreatic polypeptide
Symptoms can be nonspecific (including poor appetite/weight loss) and labs help support the diagnosis
We also check for and rule out other hormone-secreting pancreatic tumors (e.g., insulinoma, gastrinoma)
Treatment depends on location/stage; surgery is considered when resectable
Somatostatin analogs (e.g., octreotide) can help control hormone secretion and may slow tumor growth in many NETs
Key potential adverse effects of octreotide: GI upset, gallstones, and changes in blood sugar; need monitoring
Clarify goals: symptom improvement, nutritional stabilization, and tumor control/staging
Discuss need for specialist NET team and further imaging for staging
Review red flags requiring urgent care (inability to maintain intake, dehydration, severe abdominal pain, GI bleeding, new jaundice)
Nutrition strategies for poor appetite (small frequent meals, calorie/protein-dense foods, hydration)
Track weight and symptoms; report rapid weight loss or worsening appetite
If started on somatostatin analog, discuss monitoring for blood sugar changes and gallbladder symptoms

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting symptoms and timeline (weight loss/poor appetite, abdominal symptoms), functional status, and impact; screen for red flags requiring urgent care.

Collect relevant history: prior NET/MEN1 features, diabetes/hypoglycemia symptoms, peptic ulcer/GERD/diarrhea symptoms (to consider other functional PNETs), prior pancreatitis, malignancy history, family history.
Medication and supplement review (including anticoagulants, QT-prolonging agents, diabetes meds) and pregnancy status when applicable; establish baseline for interaction/contraindication checks.

Order diagnostic and baseline labs to support PPoma/PNET evaluation and safety monitoring: fasting pancreatic polypeptide, chromogranin A (with note of PPI confounding), CMP/LFTs, CBC, fasting glucose/HbA1c, +/- gastrin, insulin/C-peptide/proinsulin if symptomatic, +/- VIP/glucagon as indicated.
Interpret labs in clinical context; confirm supportive evidence of PPoma while considering false positives and alternative functional PNETs.
Synthesize findings into likely PPoma (pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor secreting pancreatic polypeptide) and note need for staging/imaging and specialist NET evaluation.
Explain diagnosis clearly, including nonspecific symptoms, role of labs, and the need to evaluate/rule out other hormone-secreting pancreatic tumors (insulinoma, gastrinoma, etc.).

Explain treatment approach: staging-dependent, surgery if resectable; discuss somatostatin analog option (octreotide) for symptom/hormone control and potential tumor growth slowing; review key adverse effects and monitoring.
Required critical safety check before prescribing any new medication (e.g., octreotide, antiemetics, acid suppression adjustments).

Check for clinically meaningful drug interactions prior to prescribing (non-critical but expected), including diabetes meds (hypo/hyperglycemia risk), bradycardia agents, and anticoagulants if procedures planned.

Initiate appropriate therapy when indicated (e.g., octreotide for symptomatic functional NET or suspected hormone-mediated symptoms) and/or supportive care (antiemetic, nutrition support), contingent on safety checks.

Address concerns: clarify goals (symptom improvement, nutritional stabilization, tumor control/staging), emphasize need for NET specialist team and imaging for staging, and provide red-flag return precautions.
Lifestyle/nutrition counseling for poor appetite/weight loss; tracking weight/symptoms; if on somatostatin analog, counsel on blood sugar monitoring and gallbladder symptom vigilance.
Arrange timely follow-up and referral coordination (GI/oncology/surgical oncology/NET center), and plan monitoring labs (glucose, LFTs) and symptom reassessment.
Close encounter after ensuring understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.
